Name: adaptive_jacobian_layer_skipping
Title: Adaptive Jacobian Layer-Skipping for Latency-Aware Mixed Precision Inference
Short Hypothesis: Can we leverage a Jacobian-based layer-skipping framework to dynamically adapt layer precision in real-time during inference, minimizing latency while maintaining model accuracy?
Related Work: Current mixed-precision methods primarily focus on static quantization strategies, such as those presented in KVTuner and Cocktail. However, these methods do not account for dynamic adaptations based on real-time inference conditions or Jacobian sensitivity analysis. This proposal introduces a dynamic layer-skipping mechanism informed by real-time Jacobian estimates, distinguishing it from existing literature which lacks this adaptive response.
Abstract: As the demand for efficient machine learning inference grows, particularly in latency-critical applications, optimizing mixed-precision schemes becomes paramount. This proposal introduces a novel adaptive Jacobian layer-skipping framework, which dynamically adjusts the precision of individual layers based on real-time Jacobian sensitivity analysis during inference. By selectively skipping layers or altering their precision, we aim to minimize latency without significantly impacting model accuracy. Our approach will be validated through a series of experiments on various architectures, benchmarking against state-of-the-art mixed-precision methods. We will employ metrics such as throughput, accuracy, and latency to evaluate performance across multiple datasets and model sizes. This research not only advances the current understanding of adaptive inference but also provides a practical solution for deploying large models in resource-constrained environments.
Experiments: 1. Implement the adaptive Jacobian layer-skipping framework in a standard deep learning model (e.g., ResNet or a Transformer).
2. Conduct experiments to assess the impact of different skipping strategies on model performance and latency, comparing static mixed-precision approaches.
3. Evaluate throughput and accuracy trade-offs using standard datasets (e.g., CIFAR-10, ImageNet).
4. Measure latency under varying hardware conditions and input sizes to assess real-time adaptability.
Risk Factors and Limitations: 1. Potential instability or accuracy drops if the Jacobian estimates are not accurate enough.
2. The need for careful tuning of thresholds for layer-skipping decisions may complicate deployment.
3. Limited generalization across diverse architectures, as the framework may need adjustments for different model types.
